coredata.dc:title: Carbon dot- and gold nanocluster-based three-channel fluorescence array sensor: Visual detection of multiple metal ions in complex samples
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Shuo
Deng, Gaoqiong
Yang, Jian
Chen, Hengye
Long, Wanjun
She, Yuanbin
Fu, Haiyan
coredata.dc:description: Heavy metal pollution has gradually become one of the most serious problems in global environmental pollution and can invade the environment, food, and medicine, eventually causing irreversible damage to the human body. Herein, we construct a multichannel visual fluorescence array sensor based on N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) and gold nanocluster (AuNCs). The sensor comprehensively considered the aggregation-induced enhanced fluorescence (AIE) effect of AuNCs, the electron transfer (ET) effect of NCDs, and the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) effect of AuNCs@NCDs. It was further combined with PLSDA and PLSR to realize rapid and accurate detection of multiple metal ions in tap water, traditional Chinese medicine (TCM), and soil. The LODs of Cd2+, Pb2+, and Hg2+ were 0.15 μmol/L, 0.20 μmol/L and 0.09 μmol/L, respectively. Simultaneously, we proposed an adjustable fluorescence spectral logic device to meet the different determination requirements towards heavy metals. The analysis result of metal ions can be directly achieved by the four output types, NOT (0, 0, 0), Cd2+ (1, 1, 0), Pb2+ (1, 0, 0) and Hg2+ (0, 0, 1). More interestingly, Cd2+ and Pb2+ can be removed by simple centrifugation or sedimentation, which can be observed through the Tyndall effect. Accordingly, we not only constructed the sensing method of multiple metal ions but also proposed a new strategy for future research on the migration of heavy metal ions from complex environments to TCM.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Gold nanocluster
Heavy metal ion
Visual detection
Logic device
Chemometrics

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of AuNCs, NCDs and AuNCs@NCDs
Detailed recognition process of Cd2+, Pb2+, and Hg2+
Detection of Cd2+, Pb2+, and Hg2+ in real samples
Characterization of AuNCs, NCDs and AuNCs@NCDs
Recognition mechanism for Cd2+, Pb2+, and Hg2+
Sensing performance of three-channel fluorescence array
Quantitative detection of various heavy metals in complex samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Authors are grateful to the financial support from the National Key R&D Program of China (No. 2020YFC1712700 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grants nos. 32122068 , 31972164 , 32001789 ), the Talented Youth Cultivation Program from “the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities ”, and South-Central Minzu University (No. CZP20007 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM characterization of AuNCs (A) and NCDS (B), with the corresponding particle size distribution as illustrated. Fluorescent lifetime of NCDs and AuNCs (C). XPS characterization results of AuNCs (D) and NCDs (E). Zeta potential characterization of AuNCs (red line) and NCDs (blue line) before and after mixing (F).
N 1s high-resolution XPS results of AuNCs and O 1s high-resolution XPS curves of NCDS before and after reaction: AuNCs (A), AuNCs+Cd2+ (B), AuNCs +Pb2+ (C), NCDs (D), and NCDs +Hg2+ (E).

FT-IR results of AuNCs before and after the reaction with Cd2+ and Pb2+ (A). FT-IR results of NCDs before and after the reaction with Hg2+ (B). FLT results of AuNCs before and after the reaction with Cd2+ and Pb2+ (C). FLT results of NCDs before and after the reaction with Hg2+ (D). τ1 and τ2 were fast and slow fluorescence lifetimes, respectively, A1 and A2 were the corresponding weight factors, and τavg was the mean fluorescence lifetime.

Tyndall effect images of AuNCs before and after reaction with Cd2+ and Pb2+ (100 μmol/L) and those of NCDs before and after reaction with Hg2+ (100 μmol/L). The beam transmission was from the bottle bottom to bottle mouth (A). Aggregation and adsorption effect of AuNCs before and after reacting with Cd2+ and Pb2+ (B).

The response of AuNCs to different metal ions: the concentrations of Cd2+ and Pb2+ were 75 μmol/L, the concentrations of Al3+ and Ag+ were 150 μmol/L, and the concentrations of other metal ions were 750 μmol/L (A). The response of AuNCs@NCDs to different metal ions: the concentration of Cd2+ was 75 μmol/L, the concentrations of Pb2+, Al3+ and Ag+ were 150 μmol/L, and the concentrations of other metal ions were 750 μmol/L (B). The response of NCDs to different metal ions: the concentration of Hg2+ was 5 μmol/L, and the concentrations of other metal ions were 100 μmol/L (C). The corresponding visualization results of three-channel array sensing: the concentrations of Cd2+, Pb2+ and Hg2+ were 75 μmol/L, and the concentrations of other metal ions were 150 μmol/L (D). Visualization results of Cd2+, Pb2+ and Hg2+ identified by three-channel fluorescence array sensing (E).
Comparison of our method with reported fluorescence sensing methods.

head.ce:title: Carbon dot- and gold nanocluster-based three-channel fluorescence array sensor: Visual detection of multiple metal ions in complex samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Modernization Engineering Technology Research Center of Ethnic Minority Medicine of Hubei Province, School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, South-Central MinZu University, Wuhan 430074, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory Breeding Base of Green Chemistry-Synthesis Technology, College of Chemical Engineering, Zhejiang University of Technology, Hangzhou 310032, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory Breeding Base of Green Chemistry-Synthesis Technology, College of Chemical Engineering, Zhejiang University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory Breeding Base of Green Chemistry-Synthesis Technology, College of Chemical Engineering, Zhejiang University of Technology, Hangzhou 310032, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Shuo Wang and Gaoqiong Deng equally contributed to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Heavy metal pollution has gradually become one of the most serious problems in global environmental pollution and can invade the environment, food, and medicine, eventually causing irreversible damage to the human body. Herein, we construct a multichannel visual fluorescence array sensor based on N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) and gold nanocluster (AuNCs). The sensor comprehensively considered the aggregation-induced enhanced fluorescence (AIE) effect of AuNCs, the electron transfer (ET) effect of NCDs, and the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) effect of AuNCs@NCDs. It was further combined with PLSDA and PLSR to realize rapid and accurate detection of multiple metal ions in tap water, traditional Chinese medicine (TCM), and soil. The LODs of Cd2+, Pb2+, and Hg2+ were 0.15 μmol/L, 0.20 μmol/L and 0.09 μmol/L, respectively. Simultaneously, we proposed an adjustable fluorescence spectral logic device to meet the different determination requirements towards heavy metals. The analysis result of metal ions can be directly achieved by the four output types, NOT (0, 0, 0), Cd2+ (1, 1, 0), Pb2+ (1, 0, 0) and Hg2+ (0, 0, 1). More interestingly, Cd2+ and Pb2+ can be removed by simple centrifugation or sedimentation, which can be observed through the Tyndall effect. Accordingly, we not only constructed the sensing method of multiple metal ions but also proposed a new strategy for future research on the migration of heavy metal ions from complex environments to TCM.

ce:section.ce:para: In the past hundred years, global heavy metal industrial development has been extremely rapid, which has inevitably caused severe pollution to the atmosphere, water quality, and soil in the process of mining, smelting, and processing [1–6]. In particular, the pollution of cadmium, lead, and mercury in food and medicine is increasingly severe, which is of broad concern in society [7–9]. Moreover, cadmium, lead, and mercury can quickly accumulate in human organs and combine with various biological enzymes in the body, causing severe digestive system disorders, urinary system disorders, and neurological injury, which are considerable threats to human life and health [10–12]. Therefore, developing a rapid detection method with high sensitivity and good selectivity is needed for people's livelihood and combat the pollution of various heavy metals in the environment, food, and medicine.

In response to the challenge of detecting cadmium, lead, and mercury, many instrumental methods to detect heavy metal ions include inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), FSP-atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS), ion mobility spectrometry (IMS), and electrochemical luminescence [13–17]. Among them, the large instrument methods have the advantages of high repeatability and good specificity but also have the disadvantages of time consumption, operation difficulty, high cost, and slow response. Furthermore, even though the electrochemical analysis method has the advantages of low cost and detection limit, its selectivity and repeatability are poor and it is difficult to achieve visual detection.

The fluorescence detection method has gained more and bolder attention because of its sensitivity, simplicity, and efficiency and has been widely used to detect target analytes [18–20]. Simultaneously, the fluorescence intensity change accompanying the colorimetric shift can provide a noticeable fluorescence color change. With the development of fluorescent probes, the fluorescence method has tremendous application potential in the field of heavy metal detection, which is ascribed to the fact that some nanomaterials used in detection systems, such as gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) and carbon dots (CDs), result in easy signal recognition by efficient and stable fluorescence intensity [21,22]. For instance, Lu et al. [23]. synthesized blue and red dual-channel dual-emission carbon dots (DDCDs) and realized dual-channel detection of Cr3+ and Pb2+ simultaneously based on ion-imprinted fluorescent polymers. Bian et al. [24]. designed a paper-based fluorescence sensor based on gold nanoclusters and realized real-time detection of Hg2+ and Pb2+. Accordingly, we speculated that the multi-channel fluorescence array sensor based on AuNCs, CDs, and their composite materials could provide more possibilities for detecting multiple metal ions, which has rarely been reported before.

Herein, we established a three-channel fluorescence array sensor based on AuNCs, NCDs, and AuNCs@NCDs to rapidly and accurately detect Cd2+, Pb2+, and Hg2+ with LODs of 0.15 μmol/L, 0.20 μmol/L, and 0.09 μmol/L, respectively. The spiked recovery in tap water, traditional Chinese medicine (TCM), and soil were ranged from 90 % to 110 % (RSD≤ 8.2 %). PLSDA and PLSR were further applied to the visual detection of various heavy metals in complex samples. Simultaneously, an adjustable fluorescence spectral logic device was proposed to meet the different heavy metal limit determination requirements. Besides, we have demonstrated that Cd2+ and Pb2+ enhanced AuNCs fluorescence via cationic-mediated AIE effect, and Hg2+ quenched NCDs fluorescence by ET effect. Interestingly, we unexpectedly found through Tyndall effect that Cd2+ and Pb2+ could be effectively removed under centrifugation or standing conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of AuNCs, NCDs and AuNCs@NCDs
ce:section.ce:para: AuNCs were synthesized by a reported method with minor modifications [25]. Briefly, 0.092 g of GSH was added into 92 mL of DDW. Then, 8 mL of freshly prepared 1 % HAuCl4 .3H2O was added into the mixture under vigorous stirring. After thorough mixing, the reaction mixture was heated to 70 °C, sealed to avoid light, and gently stirred at 1000 rpm for 20 h to obtain a clear yellow solution. Further, the solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min and filtered with a 0.22 µm microporous membrane to remove large impurities. Finally, a dialysis membrane (1000 Da) was used to remove unreacted raw materials and small molecular impurities to yield a clear AuNCs solution with orange fluorescence (Em=570 nm).

NCDs were synthesized in one step by the hydrothermal method [26]. First, 1.0 g of citric acid and 10 mg of sodium hydroxide were added to 20 mL of ultrapure water with vigorous stirring to dissolve completely. Next, 20 mL of DMF was added to the solution and sonicated for 2 min to make the mixture uniform. Then, the mixed solution was added to a polytetrafluoroethylene reactor and heated in an oven at 180 ℃ for 6 h. Further, the reaction solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 15 min, and the supernatant was taken and filtered with a 0.22 µm microporous membrane to remove impurities. Finally, blue fluorescent NCDs solution were obtained (Em=436 nm).

The synthesized AuNCs and NCDs were mixed at a certain volume ratio to produce AuNCs@NCDs composite nanomaterials by the FRET effect. AuNCs, AuNCs@NCDs, and NCDs were attached to the 96-well plate to construct three-channel visual fluorescence array sensor which was eventually used for the detection of Cd2+, Pb2+, and Hg2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detailed recognition process of Cd2+, Pb2+, and Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: All fluorescence data collection was performed at room temperature and normal pressure. The specific operation is 250 μL of the nanomaterials or solution of its composite, 500 μL of Tris-HCl buffer solution or sodium hydroxide solution, and 175 μL of DDW were added to a 1.5 mL quartz cuvette and mixed well. 75 μL of metal irons solutions such as Cd2+, Pb2+ and Hg2+ solutions of different concentrations were added so that the total volume of the reaction solution was 1 mL. The solutions were kept undisturbed for 3 min, and then the fluorescence spectra were recorded. The excitation wavelength, voltage, and slit width were 345 nm, 400 V, and 10 nm, respectively. The acquisition spectrum ranges from 400 nm to 650 nm.

In the three-channel visual fluorescence array sensing, the three channels are AuNCs, AuNCs@NCDs, and NCDs. According to the above ratio, a total 400 μL reaction solution was added to the 96-well plate. After reacting for 5 min, the fluorescent color change images before and after the nanomaterial reaction were taken with a smartphone. The RGB values of the obtained photos were extracted by Photoshop software. In the visualization RGB data, the image of each channel in the array sensor is cropped to 10 × 10 pixels in size. Hence, each channel corresponds to 100 pixels, that is, 100 sets of RGB values. Accordingly, the visualization result of each channel contains 3000 variables (10 ×300).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cd2+, Pb2+, and Hg2+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Furthermore, tap water, Chrysanthemum (CHR), Lily, Atractylodes (ATR), and Citri Reticulatae Pericarpium (CRP), and soil were used as the standard recovery samples to explore the stability and applicability of this method in TCM and the environment. Use a 0.22 µm microporous filter membrane to remove large particles of impurities in tap water; 0.1 g of CHR, Lily, ATR, CRP, and Soil powder (50 mesh) was added into 100 mL of boiling water and soaked for 15 min. Then these solutions and tap water are filtered with a 0.22 µm microporous filter membrane to remove the impurities and refrigerate it at 4 ℃ for use in the next step. All samples do not need to be processed further. Directly use the solutions prepared above as solvents to prepare metal ion solutions of different types and concentrations. Repeat the operation steps in the first paragraph of this section and combine the PLSDA and PLSR algorithms in the experiment. The fluorescence color change of the material is analyzed to achieve the accurate detection of a variety of heavy metals in TCM and the environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of AuNCs, NCDs and AuNCs@NCDs
ce:section.ce:para: This work characterized the morphology, particle size, fluorescence properties, and binding forms of AuNCs and NCDs. As shown in Fig. 1A and B, the morphology of AuNCs and NCDs was spherical and rodlike. Moreover, their particle sizes were normally distributed in the ranges of 1.69–2.59 nm and 2.51–3.34 nm, and the average particle sizes were 2.11 nm and 2.51 nm, respectively. The lattices of AuNCs and NCDs were 0.22 nm and 0.24 nm, corresponding to the (111) plane of gold and the (100) face of graphene carbon, respectively [27,28]. As shown in Fig. 1C, the fluorescence lifetime (FLT) of NCDs was nanosecond (ns) level, while that of AuNCs was microsecond (μs) level. It was known that the Au(I)-S complexes with microsecond level FLT have significant advantages in AIE effect [25], which provided favorable conditions for subsequent heavy metal detection.

Then, the existing forms of the elements of AuNCs and NCDs were characterized by XPS. As shown in Fig. 1D and Fig. S1, the seven characteristic peaks were concentrated at 85.0, 164.0, 229.0, 285.0, 334.0–353.0, 400.0, and 531.8 eV, corresponding to Au 4f, S 2p, S 2s, C 1s, Au 4d, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively [29]. As shown in Fig. 1E and Fig. S2, in the XPS results of NCDs, four characteristic peaks were concentrated at 285.0, 400.0, 497.0, and 531.8 eV, corresponding to C 1s, N 1s, Na KLL, and O 1s, respectively [30]. These results indicate that AuNCs were rich in Au, S, C, N, and O elements, while NCDs were rich in C, N, and O elements.

Further, we explored the interaction between AuNCs and NCDs by comparing their fluorescence spectra and UV–vis absorption spectra. It was found that the UV–vis absorption spectra of AuNCs partially overlapped with that of NCDs (Fig. S3A). Meanwhile, when AuNCs and NCDs were mixed, the fluorescence of NCDs was obviously quenched, while that of AuNCs was slightly enhanced (Fig. S3B), indicating a significant FRET effect between AuNCs and NCDs. To further verify the existence of the FRET effect, we further consider the result of Zeta potential. As shown in Fig. 1F, the surface of AuNCs and NCDs was negatively and positively charged, respectively, while that of AuNCs@NCDs were negatively charged, indicating that AuNCs and NCDs can bind via electrostatic interaction to narrow the spatial distance between nanoparticles and generate an effective FRET effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Recognition mechanism for Cd2+, Pb2+, and Hg2+
ce:section.ce:para: Relevant researchers have discovered the AIE effect of AuNCs [25]. However, the AIE effect based on AuNCs induced by heavy metals has not been reported in detail. Herein, the recognition mechanism of AuNCs towards heavy metals (Cd2+ and Pb2+) was first investigated by XPS, FT-IR, and FLT. From the XPS characterization results described in Fig. 2A-C, after the reaction of Cd2+ and Pb2+ with AuNCs, new peaks belonging to the N-Cd bond and N-Pb bond appeared at 405.4 eV and 406.1 eV, respectively. These indicated that Cd2+ and Pb2+ could bond with the nitrogen-containing functional group on the surface of AuNCs, resulting in a cationic-mediated AIE effect [31]. Besides, the results of FT-IR ( Fig. 3A) also supported this view. In the superposition wide peak wavenumber interval from 3000 cm−1 to 3500 cm−1, the secondary amine stretching vibration (peak at 3190 cm−1) was strengthened, indicating that more N-H bonds were generated after AuNCs reacted with Cd2+ and Pb2+. Meanwhile, the disappearance of the bending vibration of the primary amine (double peaks at 1648.27 cm−1 and 1529.24 cm−1) and the appearance of the bending vibration of the secondary amine (single peak at 1584 cm−1) also demonstrated that the decrease of H-N-H bonds and the increase of N-H bonds in AuNCs might be caused by N-Cd bonds and N-Pb bonds.

To further elucidate the AIE process of AuNCs induced by Cd2+ and Pb2+, the FLT result was discussed. As shown in Fig. 3C, the average FLT of AuNCs was 6.90 μs, while the average fluorescence lifetime after the reaction increased to 9.91 μs (for Cd2+) and 10.14 μs (for Pb2+) with increasing fluorescence intensity, respectively, indicating that the AIE process was dynamic. More surprisingly, the continuous dynamic collision of AuNCs with Cd2+ and Pb2+ can further precipitate, which provided the possibility of simultaneous detection and removal of Cd2+ and Pb2+. As shown in Fig. 1A, B, the size of AuNCs and NCDs was approximately 2 nm, making it difficult for its aqueous solution to generate light path under beam transmission. However, with the addition of Cd2+ and Pb2+, AuNCs were induced to aggregate through the combination of N-Cd and N-Pb bonds, resulting in a significant Tyndall effect ( Fig. 4A). Accordingly, simple centrifugation (2000 rpm, 3 min) or a short time (5–10 min) of sedimentation can produce fluorescence-enhanced AuNCs-Cd and AuNCs-Pb deposits (Fig. 4B). As shown in Table S1, the results of ICP-MS proved that the AIE effect of AuNCs can be used to adsorb and remove Cd2+ and Pb2+ effectively.

Moreover, this work explored the primary recognition mechanism of NCDs towards Hg2+. As shown in the result of XPS (Fig. 2D, E), a new peak appeared at 532.9 eV when Hg2+ was added into NCDs, which belonged to the O-Hg bond and indicated that Hg2+ bonded with oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of NCDs, providing conditions for the electron transfer effect. Furthermore, the result of FLT (Fig. 3D) showed that the fitting function changed from single exponential decay to double exponential decay after the addition of Hg2+. This proved that there were electron transitions from the conduction band to the surface defect of NCDs [32], which made electron transfer between NCDs and Hg2+ more likely to occur. Meanwhile, FT-IR was further applied to verify the electron transfer effect between NCDs and Hg2+. As shown in Fig. 3B, in the presence of Hg2+, the peak of the O-H stretching vibration exhibited a red-shift from 3425 cm−1 to 3381 cm−1, indicating that the electrons on the surface functional group of NCDs tend to be Hg2+ and the electron cloud density of O-H decreased. Therefore, the recognition of NCDs towards Hg2+ was realized by electron transfer.

In conclusion, the primary recognition mechanism of heavy metals in this work was that Cd2+ and Pb2+ combine with N in AuNCs to form N-Cd bonds and N-Pb bonds, which induce aggregation deposition and dynamic fluorescence enhancement of AuNCs, namely, the aggregation-induced fluorescence enhancement effect (AIE). Meanwhile, Hg2+ forms an O-Hg bond by combining with O in NCDs, which induces unpaired electrons on O to transfer to Hg2+, resulting in an electron transfer effect and static quenching of NCDs fluorescence.

In order to determine the best reaction conditions, the effects of pH of buffer solution on the identification of heavy metals by different nanomaterials were investigated. Firstly, Al3+, Cd2+, Pb2+, and Ag+ have an enhanced fluorescence effect on AuNCs in aqueous solution, and the response result is Al3+>Pb2+>Cd2+>Ag+. As the pH of the Tris-HCl buffer solution increasing, the fluorescence enhancement effect of Al3+ is gradually screened, which is because Tris is a masking agent for Al3+ and can prevent the binding of Al3+ with AuNCs (Fig. S4A). Although Ag+ showed weak fluorescence enhancement to AuNCs, it was accompanied by a significant redshift (Fig. S4B), which could be distinguished from Pb2+ and Cd2+. These results indicate that Tris-HCl buffer solution is favorable for the AIE effect between Pb2+ and Cd2+ and AuNCs with the optimal pH of 8.5. Meanwhile, Hg2+, Cu2+, and Ni2+ have a fluorescence quenching effect on AuNCs in an aqueous solution. Even with the increase of pH of Tris-HCl buffer solution, the fluorescence quenching signal between Cu2+ and Ni2+ is still difficult to distinguish (Fig. S4C). Therefore, it is challenging to determine Hg2+, Cu2+, and Ni2+ by AuNCs fluorescence quenching. In addition, Hg2+ has a good fluorescence quenching reaction on NCDs under weak base conditions, and the quenching effect is the best when pH= 9.0 (Fig. S4D, E). Therefore, 0.1 mol/L Tris-HCl buffer solution (pH=8.5) was selected as the optimal condition for AuNCs to recognize Pb2+ and Cd2+ while NaOH solution (pH=9.0) was chosen as the optimal condition for NCDs to recognize Hg2+.

Since AuNCs have similar fluorescence responses to Cd2+ and Pb2+ in Tris-HCl buffer solution, the FRET effect between AuNCs@NCDs can optimize the volume ratio (AuNCs: NCDs) to distinguish Cd2+ from Pb2+. As shown in Fig. S5A, with the increase of the proportion of NCDs, the response of Pb2+ to AuNCs@NCDs gradually decreased and there is no response when the volume ratio is 1: 1. It's possibly due to the FRET effect between AuNCs and NCDs through electrostatic interaction, which shields the fluorescence enhancement of AuNCs by Pb2+ and makes AuNCs@NCDs highly selective for Cd2+. However, in visual sensing, the volume ratio of AuNCs@NCDs still needs to be further optimized to achieve the best visualization effect. As shown in Fig. S5B, AuNCs@NCDs with different volume ratios can produce different degrees of ratio fluorescence color change before and after the reaction with Cd2+. Therefore, Lab color space is introduced in this work to evaluate the degree of visual color variation of ratio fluorescence sensing [33]. Lab color space is a kind of CIE XYZ color space coordinate based on nonlinear compression, where dimension "L" represents brightness, and "a" and "b" represent color opposites. The chromaticity value ∆E is calculated by Formula S1 to indicate the degree of visual color change, and the larger the value, the better the visualization effect. As shown in Table S10, when the volume of AuNCs: NCDs= 1:2, ∆E is maximum (∆E = 51.6), and the visualization effect of AuNCs@NCDs to recognize Cd2+ is the best. Therefore, the volume ratio of 1:2 is finally selected as the optimal condition for AuNCs@NCDs to recognize Cd2+.
∆E=[(L1-L2)2+(a1-a2)2+(b1-b2)2]1/2 (Formula S1)

In the process of Cd2+ recognition by AuNCs@NCDs, the fluorescence intensity of NCDs at 436 nm gradually decreased with the increase of reaction time (Fig. S5C). This is because AuNCs-Cd deposited into larger particles and accumulated during the reaction process, resulting in a smaller gap between AuNCs and NCDs, which promoted the FRET effect between AuNCs and NCDs, causing a constant decrease in fluorescence intensity of NCDs. Further study showed that the reduction of fluorescence intensity at 436 nm had little effect on the visual fluorescence color change of AuNCs@NCDs to recognize Cd2+ in 0–15 min that is why the ratio fluorescence (F570/F436) was not selected in this work. In comparison, the fluorescence intensity at 570 nm is used to represent the response signal of AuNCs@NCDs.

Under the optimal reaction conditions mentioned above, the reaction time of AuNCs to recognize Cd2+ and Pb2+, AuNCs@NCDs to recognize Cd2+, and NCDs to recognize Hg2+ were optimized. As shown in Fig. S5D, the reactions of different nanomaterials with Cd2+, Pb2+, and Hg2+ were basically stable within 5 min, indicating that the sensing method could quickly identify Cd2+, Pb2+, and Hg2+, and the reaction time of 5 min was the best reaction condition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing performance of three-channel fluorescence array
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal reaction condition, the selectivity of the proposed method was evaluated by reacting different nanomaterials (AuNCs, NCDs, and AuNCs@NCDs) with various metal ions (Cd2+, Pb2+, Al3+, Ag+, Ca2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, and Sn2+) in this work. As shown in Fig. 5A, AuNCs exhibited good fluorescence enhancement only for Cd2+ and Pb2+, and no reaction or fluorescence quenching for other heavy metals, indicating that AuNCs have good selectivity for Cd2+ and Pb2+. Fig. 5B showed that AuNCs@NCDs had an excellent fluorescence enhancement response to Cd2+, indicating that AuNCs@NCDs displayed good selectivity for Cd2+. Fig. 5C demonstrated that NCDs had good selectivity to Hg2+ through fluorescence quenching. As shown in the visualization results (Fig. 5D, E), AuNCs, AuNCs@NCDs, and NCDs were arranged from left to right, constructing a "1 × 3" three-channel visual fluorescence array sensor where the fluorescence enhancement caused by Cd2+ and Pb2+ and fluorescence quenching caused by Hg2+ can be effectively distinguished from other metal ions. Therefore, the proposed three-channel visual fluorescence array was feasible to detect Cd2+, Pb2+, and Hg2+.

According to the above optimal conditions, the fluorescence intensities of AuNCs with gradient concentrations of Cd2+ and Pb2+, AuNCs@NCDs with gradient concentrations of Cd2+, and NCDs with gradient concentrations of Hg2+ were measured. Then, the concentration of heavy metals (C) was taken as the x-coordinate. The fluorescence intensity ratio F1/F0 before and after the reaction was taken as the y-coordinate to construct the corresponding standard curve. As shown in Fig. S6A, B and Fig. S7A, B, the fluorescence intensity (F570) of AuNCs and AuNCs@NCDs gradually increased after the reaction with a gradient concentration of Cd2+, showing a good linear relationship between 0.38-338 μmol/L and 0.38–225 μmol/L, respectively. Furthermore, the LODs were 0.15 μmol/L and 0.29 μmol/L, respectively (3σ/KSV, n = 3). Therefore, the linear regression equation fitted by the Stern-Volmer equation can be expressed as Formulas 1, 2, and 3:
(Formula 1) F1/F0 =5·906×10−2[CCd]+0·909
(Formula 2) F1/F0 =6·474×10−3[CCd]+4·950
(Formula 3) F1/F0 =2·759×10−2[CCd]+1·011

As shown in Fig. S6C and Fig. S7C, the fluorescence intensity of AuNCs gradually increased after the reaction with a gradient concentration of Pb2+, offering a good linear relationship between 0.75 and 60 μmol/L with a LOD of 0.20 μmol/L. The fitted linear regression equation can be expressed as Formula 4:
(Formula 4) F1/F0=8·093×10−2[CPb]+0·989

As shown in Fig. S6D and Fig. S7D, after the reaction with gradient concentrations of Hg2+, the fluorescence intensity of NCDs was gradually quenched, offering an excellent linear relationship between 0.15 and 7.5 μmol/L, with a LOD of 0.09 μmol/L. The fitted linear regression equation can be expressed as Formula 5:
(Formula 5) F1/F0=−8·738×10−2[CHg]+0·934

According to the above fitted linear regression equation, the exact quantification of Cd2+, Pb2+, and Hg2+ can be realized. Compared with other reported fluorescence sensing methods ( Table 1), this method has a more comprehensive detection range and a relatively low detection limit for Cd2+, Pb2+, and Hg2+.

Based on the rapid identification of various heavy metals by fluorescence spectroscopy, a logic device was designed to meet the requirements of heavy metal exceedance determination for different detection purposes in practical applications. The principle of this device was to convert the fluorescence intensity signal of the nano effect fluorescence spectra into a digital signal and distinguish the fluorescence intensity signal exceeding the threshold value as "1" or "0". Then, different threshold values were set according to different limit requirements to achieve different detection purposes, which were used to determine Cd2+, Pb2+, and Hg2+. The design idea was shown in Fig. S8. For example, the threshold values of AuNCs, AuNCs@NCDs, and NCDs for the detection of Cd2+, Pb2+ and Hg2+ were set to 1.2, 1.2, and 1.1 times their original fluorescence, respectively, and the fluorescence intensity changes were represented by F1/F0, F1/F0, and F0/F1. Then, the threshold concentration of different heavy metals under various threshold adjustments was obtained with the corresponding linear fitting equation. Under these conditions, Cd2+ and Pb2+ with concentrations greater than 4.9 μmol/L and 2.9 μmol/L were identified as "1" by AuNCs channel. A concentration of greater than 6.5 μmol/L Cd2+ detected by the AuNCs@NCDs channel will be determined as "1". The concentration of Hg2+ detected by the NCDs channel was much greater than 0.28 μmol/L, which was identified as "1". In contrast, other metal ions were identified as "0". Therefore, the determination result of the blank group and other metal ions was (0, 0, 0), while the results of Cd2+, Pb2+, and Hg2+ exceeding the limit were (1, 1, 0), (1, 0, 0) and (0, 0, 1), respectively. This method can distinguish other metal ions from Cd2+, Pb2+, and Hg2+, and the three target metal ions are also clearly distinguished.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative detection of various heavy metals in complex samples
ce:section.ce:para: We investigated the applicability of the sensing method in complex samples by detecting Cd2+, Pb2+, and Hg2+ in tap water, CHR, ATR, Lily, CRP, and soil. As shown in Table S2, Table S3, Table S4, and Table S5, the spiked recovery of Cd2+, Pb2+, and Hg2+ in complex samples was in the range of 90 %− 110 %, and the relative standard deviation was less than 8.2 %. These results indicated that the three-channel fluorescence array based on AuNCs, NCDs, and AuNCs@NCDs for detecting Cd2+, Pb2+, and Hg2+ was basically not disturbed by the complex samples with satisfactory identification and quantitative results.

To improve the portability of the sensing method and realize the purpose of visualizing the rapid identification of various heavy metals, a visual fluorescence array sensor based on three-channel was further developed to explore its applicability to detect various heavy metals in complex samples. As shown in Fig. S9, AuNCs and AuNCs@NCDs showed obvious fluorescent color changes (turn-on) with the gradual increase of Cd2+ concentration (0, 3.75, 15, 30, 52.5, and 75 μmol/L). With the gradual increase in Pb2+ (0, 3.75, 15, 30, 45, and 75 μmol/L)concentration, AuNCs produced an obvious fluorescent color change (turn-on). With the gradual increase in Hg2+ concentration (0, 0.75, 7, 15, 30, and 45 μmol/L), AuNCs@NCDs and NCDs produced significant fluorescence color changes. These results indicated that the three-channel visual fluorescence array sensor could produce a rich and obvious fluorescence color change response with gradient concentrations of Cd2+, Pb2+, and Hg2+ in tap water, CHR, ATR, Lily, CRP, and soil accompanied by certain rules.

Furthermore, the RGB value of the visual color change signal was extracted by Photoshop software, and each pixel in the RBG image was converted into the corresponding data array combined with the image preprocessing function chemometrics. Then, PLSDA and PLSR were used for analysis. In the complex samples of tap water, CHR, ATR, Lily, CRP, and soil, there were 6 concentrations of each heavy metal and 3 samples of each concentration; 108 samples of each heavy metal were obtained. The above research proved that the detection results of heavy metals in different complex samples by three-channel visual fluorescence array sensor were consistent. Therefore, the concentration of different heavy metals in various complex samples obtained by the visual sensor was directly randomly divided into a training set and prediction set (Table S6). The virtual code of six concentrations of heavy metals in complex samples (Table S7) are, respectively, f 01,(1,0,0,0,0,0), f 02,(0,1,0,0,0,0), f 03,(0,0,1,0,0,0), f 04,(0,0,0,1,0,0), f 05,(0,0,0,0,1,0) and f 06(0, 0,0,0,0,0,1), which was pattern recognition based on the position of maximum virtual encoding.

The discriminant results of PLSDA were shown in Table S8. In complex samples, three-channel visual fluorescence array sensing can all achieve 100 % discrimination for Cd2+, Pb2+, and Hg2+ with high sensitivity and strong specificity, indicating that this method can be used to discriminate the concentrations of Cd2+, Pb2+, and Hg2+. Furthermore, PLSR pattern recognition was used to correlate the RGB value of the fluorescent color of the nanomaterial with the concentrations of Cd2+, Pb2+, and Hg2+. Eight cross-validations were used to determine that the best-hidden variable of the models was 6. The root mean square error of correction (RMSEC) and root means a square error of prediction (RMSEP) were used to evaluate the accuracy of the correction model. R2c and R2p were used to assess the coefficient of determination of the actual concentration in the training set and the predicted concentration in the prediction set. The discriminant results of this method for different concentrations of Cd2+, Pb2+, and Hg2+ in complex samples were shown in Table S9. The root means the square error was not less than 1.3300, and the correlation coefficient between the actual and predicted concentrations was higher than 0.9987. Meanwhile, the correlation curves between the actual concentrations of Cd2+, Pb2+, and Hg2+ and the predicted concentrations in different complex samples were shown in Fig. S10. In complex samples, the predicted concentrations of Cd2+, Pb2+, and Hg2+ can form an excellent linear relationship with the actual concentrations without deviation.

These results indicated that the proposed fluorescent color-varying RGB data combined with PLSDA and PLSR models could accurately identify different concentrations of Cd2+, Pb2+, and Hg2+ in actual samples of the environment and TCM, providing accurate and reliable quantitative results. Meanwhile, a new potential method for heavy metal migration from complex environments to TCM.

In summary, we synthesized AuNCs and NCDs in one step by a hydrothermal method and simultaneously obtained AuNCs@NCDs complexes by the FRET effect. Then a three-channel fluorescence assay sensing method was constructed to accurately detect Cd2+, Pb2+, and Hg2+ in TCM and complex environments. The LODs were 0.15 μmol/L, 0.20 μmol/L, and 0.09 μmol/L, which ultimately met the national standard requirements (GB2762–2017). At the same time, a logic device based on the fluorescence spectra was designed to meet the needs of the different heavy metal limit determination under various threshold adjustments. Combined with PLSDA and PLSR, the three-channel fluorescence array sensor was used for visual identification of different metal ions in complex samples with a satisfactory result. We found that Cd2+ and Pb2+ can combine with AuNCs, resulting in the formation of complexes exhibiting the Tyndall effect, which could be precipitated and removed by simple centrifugation or sedimentation. The proposed new method can meet the requirements of visual and instrument-free rapid identification of Cd2+, Pb2+, and Hg2+, providing a new strategy for detecting and removing a variety of heavy metals in TCM and complex environments. Meanwhile, a potential method for heavy metal migration from TCM complex environments towards TCM was proposed.

Shuo Wang: Investigation, Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Gaoqiong Deng: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Jian Yang: Funding acquisition, Resources. Hengye Chen: Data curation, Formal analysis. Wanjun Long: Investigation, Software. Yuanbin She: Investigation, Supervision. Haiyan Fu: Conceptualization, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are grateful to the financial support from the National Key R&D Program of China (No. 2020YFC1712700), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grants nos. 32122068, 31972164, 32001789), the Talented Youth Cultivation Program from “the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities”, and South-Central Minzu University (No. CZP20007).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2022.132194.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

Org. Lett.

Microchem. J.

Liang, Facile preparation of amino-carbon dots/gold nanoclusters FRET ratiometric fluorescent probe for sensing of Pb2+/Cu2+, Sensors Actuators, B Chem. 282 (2019) 78–84. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.11.058.

Dye. Pigment.

Talanta

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescence enhancement of bovine serum albumin gold nanoclusters from La3+ ion: detection of four divalent metal ions (Hg2+, Cu2+, Pb2+ and Cd2+)
J. Mol. Liq.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.L. Desai, H. Basu, S. Saha, R.K. Singhal, S.K. Kailasa, Fluorescence enhancement of bovine serum albumin gold nanoclusters from La3+ ion: Detection of four divalent metal ions (Hg2+, Cu2+, Pb2+ and Cd2+), J. Mol. Liq. 336 (2021) 116239. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molliq.2021.116239.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Shuo Wang receivd his Master degree from South-Central MinZu University under the supervision of Prof. Haiyan Fu. His research mainly focuses on the application of new methods for food security through nanometer materials and spectroscopic sensors.

Gaoqiong Deng receivd her Bachelor degree from South-Central MinZu University in 2018 and she is a Master student supervised by Prof. Haiyan Fu in from South-Central MinZu University Her research mainly focuses on the application of new methods for food security through nanometer materials and spectroscopic sensors.

Jian Yang received his doctorate degree from Northwest A&F University in 2011. In 2015, he graduated from the Postdoctoral Research station of Institute of Chinese Materia Medica, China Academy of Chinese Medical Sciences. He is currently working at the Chinese Medicine Resource Center of China Academy of Chinese Medical Sciences. He presided over or participated in 7 national, provincial and ministerial projects, such as key special projects of key RESEARCH and development plan of The Ministry of Science and Technology, Basic Science and Technology Special projects, National Natural Science Foundation of China, and capacity building project of sustainable Utilization of Precious Traditional Chinese medicine resources, and participated in the research and development of 3 and 5 categories of major new drugs. His research involves the analysis and quality evaluation of Chinese herbal ingredients.

Hnegye Chen receivd his Bachelor degree from Huazhong Agricultural University in 2012 and obtained Ph.D. degree under the supervision of Prof. Fusheng Chen in the College of Food Science & Technology of Huazhong Agricultural University in 2018. From 2016.10–2017.10, he worked as a joint Ph.D. student in University of Geneva (Switzerland). Currently, he joined Prof. Haiyan Fu’s research group at School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, South-Central MinZu University. His research involves in fluorescence sensors and rapid detection methods for food safety.

Wanjun Long received her Bachelor degree from Hengyang Normal University in 2016 and obtained Ph.D. degree under the supervision of Prof. Hai-Long Wu in the College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering of Hunan University in 2021. She joined Prof. Haiyan Fu’s research group at School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, South-Central Minzu University. Her research involves in fluorescence sensors and rapid detection methods for food safety.

Prof. Yuanbin She is the dean and professor of College of Chemical Engineering at Zhejiang University of Technology (P. R. China). He received his Ph.D. in engineering from Dalian University of Technology (P. R. China) in 1997. He then worked at Beijing University of Technology (P. R. China) until 2014 before moving to Zhejiang University of Technology. His research mainly focuses on the molecule design, synthesis and application of metalloporphyrins and biometric catalysis.

Prof. Haiyan Fu is the dean and professor of School of Pharmaceutical Sciences at South-central Minzu University. She received her Ph.D. in analytical chemistry from Hunan University in 2010. Then she works in the School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, South-Central MinZu University and became a full professor in 2017. Her research mainly focuses on the development and application of new methods for drug and food security through chemometrics combined with nanometer materials and spectroscopic techniques. Prof. Fu has over 100 publications, including Biosen. Bioelectron., Carbon, Sensor Actuat. B: Chem., Anal. Chem., Food Chem., Anal. Chim. Acta., etc.
